Replicated the moment with a special edition can within 48 hours

extracted.tagline: The kind of sponsorship fans actually want
extracted.context: Brazilians care deeply about football jerseys. In recent years, clubs have added up to 25 sponsor logos to their kits to fend off economic problems, ruining iconic designs and angering fans.
extracted.idea: Consul sponsored Juventus (Brazil) but bought up every logo space on the jersey and left it empty — giving fans a clean kit with no visual interferences, including their own logo.
execution: Bought all sponsor spaces on Juventus club jersey
Removed every logo (including Consul's own) for a clean jersey
Worn across 20 official games in the entire 2024 season
In-stadium and broadcast presence during matches
press_coverage: "Clean Sponsorship proves there's more to sponsorship than meets the eye" - Lance!
"Consul logo is nowhere to be seen and yet it is now on everyone's mind" - Placar
"Not showing their (and any) logo made Consul win the hearts of every football fan" - Estadão

inferred.insight: Fans don't want more sponsor logos on their beloved jerseys — they want fewer. A sponsor that respects the jersey wins more love than one that brands it.
inferred.one_liner: The sponsorship IS the absence of sponsorship.
inferred.qualitative_summary: By buying every logo slot on a beloved Brazilian club's jersey and leaving it blank, Consul restored an iconic kit to its fans and reframed sponsorship as an act of respect rather than clutter, earning massive cultural goodwill and media attention without ever showing its logo.

visual.style_description: Editorial case-study layout with a clear before/after centerpiece of two jerseys, framed by structured columns of body copy, pull quotes, match photography grid, and metrics. Clean typographic hierarchy with a spray-paint headline treatment evoking the 'clean' concept.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Sightwalks expanded the universal language of tactile paving into a destination-finding system, giving visually impaired Peruvians independence in Miraflores and creating an open-source urban standard ready for global adoption.

inferred.one_liner: An unknown granny becomes Spain's biggest subway star.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned an anonymous Galician grandmother into a nationally recognised cultural figure, generating press, social chatter and cross-border conversation that proved out-of-home's pulling power and convinced advertisers to reinvest in subway media.

extracted.tagline: The helpline that turns the object of addiction into a way out.
extracted.context: Every 6 minutes, someone in the US dies from opioids, yet opioids remain widely accessible for medical reasons. If people can't be prevented from getting pills, how can they be prevented from abusing them?
extracted.idea: A helpline that converts the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions (e.g. codeine IP33 dialled as 4733), connecting callers to the recorded story of someone who quit that exact pill plus live expert advice.
execution: Contextual OOH billboards featuring real pill imprint codes
Website (855-how-to-quit.org) with audio stories like 'How Jason Quit'
Mobile/web experience with recorded recovery testimonials
Social media activism
PSA on national TV
Film content showing callers and former addicts who answer
Bench and street-level contextual placements
metrics: 142 people offered to share recovery stories
6.1M+ Americans suffer from opioid use disorder (context stat)

inferred.insight: Every opioid pill carries a mandatory imprint code — a unique identifier already in the addict's hand at the moment of greatest temptation.
inferred.one_liner: The pill in your hand becomes the helpline number.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned the very object of addiction into a direct line to recovery, sparking thousands of calls and a wave of survivors willing to share their stories within weeks of launch. The campaign reframed pill imprint codes as a public health asset and earned broad editorial coverage across major US news outlets.

visual.style_description: Documentary-style photography combined with bold sans-serif typography and real pill props embedded into headlines; layout is editorial and grid-based with contextual OOH imagery anchoring the case.

extracted.context: Intellectual freedom is under attack in America. More books than ever are being forced off library shelves. Over the last year, thousands of libraries have been forced to ban books in 21 states.
extracted.idea: Using PEN America's Index of Book Bans, DPLA built the most up-to-date database of banned books and geofenced the exact footprint of every library with banned books, so anyone walking into those libraries gains instant access to the banned titles.
execution: Geofenced 2,314 libraries (e.g. Central York, Pennsylvania with 161 banned books located)
Book unbanned automatically after entering a library
Launched with President Obama
Students/readers accessing banned books on their phones
Hyper-local media trucks in 21 states ('Texas banned 438 books, we unbanned all of them')
Supported by major publishers (HarperCollins, Penguin Random House, Hachette)
press_coverage: The Guardian – 'Obama backs The Banned Book Club to fight censorship'
The New York Times – 'How Libraries Are Fighting Book Banning'

inferred.insight: A library's physical walls are also its censorship boundary—cross the threshold and the ban could be lifted with a phone.
inferred.one_liner: Every censoring library becomes a portal to the banned books.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed thousands of compromised libraries into unwitting distribution points for the very books they were forced to remove, drawing endorsement from President Obama and major publishers while reigniting a national conversation on censorship and intellectual freedom.

visual.style_description: Dense, dossier-style case board built around a central hero shot of a hand holding a phone displaying the redacted book cover, surrounded by grids of blacked-out book pages and flanked by stat columns and press logos. Tone is journalistic and activist, evoking censorship through heavy black bars against acid-green accents.

file_path: 2024/OUTDOOR/SHORTLIST/Cashback for your Jersey_s Back - Flash (FCB Brasil, Sao Paulo).jpeg

extracted.tagline: We can't put our brand on the Brazil jersey, but fans just do it.
extracted.context: Nike's Brazil FIFA World Cup jersey was released costing R$350, the equivalent of 1/3 of the Brazilian minimum wage, leaving fans outraged.
extracted.idea: Flash turned Brazilian fans into live billboards by offering cashback to those who customized their Brazil jerseys with the Flash brand name and posted them on social media.
execution: Step 1: Fans customize the Brazil jersey on Nike's e-commerce with the word FLASH
Step 2: Fans post the customized jersey on social media tagging the brand
Step 3: Fans receive cashback as a virtual voucher via DM
Visual grid of fan-posted Instagram photos wearing yellow and blue Brazil jerseys printed with 'FLASH'
Second strip showing handmade customizations after Nike prohibited the word FLASH on its e-commerce

inferred.insight: Fans couldn't afford the official jersey, and brands couldn't officially sponsor it — but fans control what goes on their own backs.
inferred.one_liner: Fans' jersey backs become Flash's billboard.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Flash inserted itself into World Cup conversations without paying for official sponsorship by turning frustrated Brazilian fans into willing brand ambassadors, generating massive earned media and forcing Nike to react by banning the word on its customization platform.

visual.style_description: Dark case-study board with bold pink and cream display typography anchored by a hero shot of the yellow Brazil jersey, supported by a dense grid of Instagram screenshots showing real fan posts. Composition is clean and editorial, balancing product hero with social proof mosaic.

extracted.tagline: The first digital billboard to become a city attraction.
extracted.insight: People love to catch icons like the Eiffel Tower, the Pyramids, or Statue of Liberty.
extracted.idea: Playing on the universal behavior of catching icons in photos, Coca-Cola gave people a worldwide icon to catch on digital billboards: the Coke bottle. Catching it and sharing it online earned a free Coke.
execution: Digital billboards in Madrid, Milan and Bucharest featuring a falling Coca-Cola bottle
On-screen instructions: Catch it, Post it #CatchTheCoke, Get a free Coca-Cola
People posing in creative positions to 'catch' the bottle with their hands
User-generated social posts on Instagram/TikTok using #CatchTheCoke
Free Coca-Cola reward redeemed at the activation site
metrics: 241x longer than average time spent in front of an OOH
Most shared billboard ever in Romania, Spain and Italy

inferred.context: Out-of-home advertising is typically passive and ignored, while tourists worldwide instinctively pose for forced-perspective photos with global landmarks.
inferred.one_liner: The Coke bottle becomes the landmark you pose with.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Transformed standard digital billboards into city attractions across three European capitals, turning passive OOH viewers into active participants who lingered, played, and shared. Became the most shared billboard ever in Romania, Spain and Italy, dramatically extending dwell time and amplifying Coke's social engagement.

visual.style_description: Cinematic night photography centered on a smiling young woman posing to 'catch' a giant illuminated Coke bottle on a towering red digital billboard, flanked by a collage of UGC phone screens. Dense informational layout with clean white sans-serif typography on black, balancing hero image, body copy, pictogram icons, and a filmstrip of activation stills.

file_path: 2024/OUTDOOR/SHORTLIST/Climatechangetown.pk - Government of Sindh _ SPHF (Impact BBDO, Dubai).jpeg

extracted.brand: Sindh Peoples Housing for Flood Affectees (SPHF) / Government of Sindh
extracted.tagline: To help rebuild a flood-affected town, we gave it the name of the donation website URL.
extracted.context: In 2022, Pakistan experienced its worst-ever flooding caused by climate change. The massive floods destroyed lives and washed away millions of homes.
extracted.idea: To raise funds for rebuilding flood-shattered communities, SPHF and the Government of Sindh officially renamed a rebuilt town after the donation website URL: ClimateChangeTown.pk, unveiled at COP 28.
execution: Official town signpost in Urdu and English reading 'ClimateChangeTown.pk'
Government decree/document officially approving the town's new name
Roadside distance marker '6 Km Climate Change Town.PK'
Road signage along highways pointing to the town
Brick-by-brick reconstruction of homes with local laborers
Women-led construction work on site
Before/after photos of destroyed vs. rebuilt family homes
Donation website (ClimateChangeTown.pk) mapped as a real geographic location
press_coverage: SciTech News — 'Puts climate change on the map.'

inferred.insight: Donation website URLs are forgettable, but a place name on a map is permanent and impossible to ignore.
inferred.one_liner: The town's name IS the donation URL.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a flood-ravaged Pakistani town into a permanent, government-sanctioned billboard for climate donations, anchoring the world's largest housing reconstruction program and putting climate-change accountability literally on the map.

visual.style_description: Documentary photography of villagers beside the official town signpost, framed inside a giant browser search bar to mirror the URL concept. Dense case-study layout with before/after strips, government documents, and large serif metrics over a map background.

execution: Exhibition & debate at art museums
Print ads in newspapers
OOH placements with remastered landscape paintings
Augmented reality experience via smartphone
Geotargeted ads addressing specific Danish regions
10,000+ posters distributed
metrics: 839,000 reflected on the importance of the green transition
484,000 Danes became more interested in wind energy
press_coverage: Politiken: 'This year's most successful piece of advertisement'
DR P1: 'The standstill in our green energy production might be found in our romantic landscape depictions.'

inferred.one_liner: Iconic Danish landscape paintings, remastered with wind turbines.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed wind turbines as part of Denmark's cherished landscape heritage rather than its destroyer, shifting public sentiment toward wind energy and earning national press as the year's most successful ad.

visual.style_description: Museum-inspired case board layered over a soft-focus photograph of a classical art gallery facade, with a horizontal strip of remastered romantic landscape paintings as the hero. Elegant serif typography and italic accents evoke a fine-art catalogue.

file_path: 2024/OUTDOOR/SHORTLIST/Faces of the Future - Rexona (VML, Buenos Aires).jpeg

extracted.tagline: It might be the end of the Women's World Cup. But it's just the beginning.
extracted.context: Many girls drop out of football due to lack of role models, as women's football visibility largely exists only every four years during the FIFA Women's World Cup. Rexona, as official sponsor, wanted to change that.
extracted.insight: After the final, there was a chance to challenge people to keep supporting women's football all the time, not only during the World Cup. While the world watched the players, Rexona looked at the girls being inspired by them.
extracted.idea: Using real, unposed stock images captured by FIFA during the World Cup, the campaign showcased the genuine emotions of girls whose dreams are just beginning, emphasizing that the end of the World Cup sets a new chapter.
execution: Global campaign launched the day after the World Cup final
Newspaper ads
Billboards placed near local football clubs where girls train
Social media activation
OOH placements shown in Barcelona (Spain), Rio de Janeiro (Brazil), Buenos Aires (Argentina) and Sydney (Australia)
metrics: +55 million shares of #Rexona during the World Cup
+3 million girls joined the sport after the World Cup

inferred.one_liner: The fans in the stands are the next World Cup.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Shifted the World Cup narrative from players to the next generation of girls watching them, driving millions of new players into the sport and extending Rexona's FIFA partnership through 2027.

visual.style_description: Photojournalistic campaign layout on black, featuring candid, unposed stadium photography of young girls reacting to players, paired with elegant serif/sans headline typography. Dense case-study composition with a filmstrip of supporting moments beneath the hero images.

extracted.tagline: Faster Food
extracted.context: McDonald's is available for delivery on DoorDash and on its two main competitor apps. The brief was to distinguish DoorDash from the pack.
extracted.idea: Speak the language of McDonald's iconography by abstracting the fillings of its most beloved sandwiches, stretching them horizontally across media to visualize how DoorDash delivers fast food even faster.
execution: 3D burger cube installation in a major transit hub
Horizontal OOH billboards with stretched burger fillings
Subway/transit station wall placements
Outdoor large-format billboards in city environments
press_coverage: Fast Company: "Fast food advertising is in its art school phase"

inferred.insight: McDonald's burger fillings are so iconic they're recognizable even when abstracted into pure color stripes.
inferred.one_liner: Big Macs stretched into horizontal stripes of speed.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Distinguished DoorDash from rival delivery apps by turning McDonald's most recognizable sandwiches into beautiful motion-blurred abstractions, earning widespread design press coverage and positioning DoorDash as the faster McDelivery option.

visual.style_description: Bold, cinematic case-study layout anchored by a giant 3D burger cube installation, surrounded by horizontally stretched stripe abstractions of burger fillings. Confident sans-serif typography and dark transit-hub backdrop give it a polished, art-directed feel.

extracted.tagline: The World's First Football Pitch to Have a Female Gender
extracted.context: Young girls in the Middle East grow up believing sports aren't for women, and most give up sports by age 14. Ironically, in Arabic — which predates these cultural beliefs — most sports-related words have a female gender.
extracted.insight: In Arabic, the female gender of a word is created by adding a letter at the end: ة (Ta' Marbuta) — a circle with two dots on top.
extracted.idea: In partnership with Onyx FC (UAE's first all-female owned and operated football team), two dots were added above the center circle of their pitch, turning it into a Ta' Marbuta and creating the world's first football pitch with a female gender.
execution: Aerial view of the football pitch transformed with two dots above the center circle to form the Arabic Ta' Marbuta
Onyx FC purple kit and club crest featuring female players
Players on the modified pitch during training/matches
Photo strip showing other community sports fields and courts later modified with the same two-dot symbol across Dubai

inferred.one_liner: The football pitch becomes feminine in Arabic.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed a male-dominated sporting space through Arabic grammar itself, inspiring communities across Dubai to replicate the two-dot mark on their own fields and signalling that girls have always belonged in sport.

visual.style_description: Hero aerial 'tiny planet' photograph of the pitch anchors a dense editorial case-study layout, flanked by supporting photography, Arabic typography specimens, and clean sans-serif body copy on a deep navy background.

extracted.tagline: We replaced our drivers with real psychologists to help the mental health of young people
extracted.context: In 2024 in France, 1 in 5 young people under 30 show symptoms of depression. Mental health remains stigmatized, with many believing 'doctors are for the weak, therapists are for the crazy'.
extracted.insight: At Heetch, 75% of clients are under 30, and rides create a conducive environment for open conversation.
extracted.idea: Heetch partnered with the Falret Foundation to replace some drivers with real psychologists, turning rides into safe one-to-one therapy sessions free from judgment.
execution: Ride-sharing app Heetch offers therapy with every trip
At the end of each ride, passengers paid as if for a regular psychology session
Passengers received an official receipt and a link to a dedicated platform to continue therapy with the closest therapist to their home
Visual strip showing multiple psychologist-drivers and passengers in Heetch cars

inferred.one_liner: Your Heetch driver is a therapist.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Heetch lowered the psychological and financial barrier to therapy for young people in France by reframing a routine ride-share trip as a first session with a real psychologist, normalising mental health conversations while driving subscriber growth and rides for the brand.

visual.style_description: Cinematic in-car photograph shot from the back seat, showing a young passenger in conversation with a psychologist-driver, overlaid with pink neuron-like vein illustrations and bold white-and-pink typography. Information-dense case-study layout with side text blocks, KPI bar, and a film-strip of additional driver-passenger pairs.

extracted.tagline: A series of billboards that change every time people share a meal
extracted.context: Only ~30% of families eat together regularly and the average family dinner lasts just 12 minutes (Harvard/NPR). Coca-Cola wanted to celebrate the magic of shared meals.
extracted.idea: Special frames placed in restaurants that turn real people sharing meals into ever-changing Coca-Cola billboards, celebrating real connections at mealtime.
execution: Red Coca-Cola frames installed on restaurant windows turning diners into live billboards
Each billboard captioned with diners' names, time and relationship (e.g., 'Basilio and Carlos, Childhood friends')
Multiple restaurant placements across cities shown in thumbnail strip with framed windows of various shapes

inferred.insight: The magic of Coca-Cola has always lived in real human connections shared around a table, not in staged advertising imagery.
inferred.one_liner: The restaurant window IS the Coca-Cola billboard.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned ordinary restaurant diners into the brand's outdoor campaign, replacing staged advertising with authentic shared moments and measurably extending how long people lingered at the table.

visual.style_description: Cinematic documentary-style photography with warm interior lighting viewed through rainy restaurant windows, framed by bold red Coca-Cola borders that mimic billboard structure. Editorial layout with bracketed serif headlines and clean supporting copy.

extracted.tagline: Get Comfortable

execution: Celebrity Big Brother 2024 ITV partnership
Contextual digi-van campaign across London landmarks
National TV reaching 29 million
National OOH campaign
Waterloo Station takeover of Britain's busiest toilet
Social activity on X with #GetComfortable
metrics: +£3.2M retail sales vs same period last year
+36.3% record market share over the past ten years
+122% search increase for the signs of bowel cancer

extracted.tagline: An activation that highlights the missing opportunities for women in football
extracted.context: Women in football don't get the same opportunities as men: fewer facilities, sponsorships, coaches, airtime and salary. These inequalities affect self-esteem and joy, and create a public image that women aren't good enough.
extracted.idea: An interactive outdoor brand activation featuring three life-sized football goals with data-driven gaps and bent crossbars that visualise the unequal opportunities for women in Dutch football.
execution: Three data-driven goals: 59% smaller (coaches), 54% smaller (sponsorships), 67% smaller (investments)
Launched during the Women's World Cup as centrepiece of integrated campaign
Special matches with kids and influencers
Goals placed outside stadiums and sent to TV studios
DOOH campaign and nationwide tour with unfair matches
TVC with Dutch national team players
Influencer content and Snapchat game lens

inferred.insight: Gender inequality in football is invisible in statistics but becomes undeniable when you see it in physical form.
inferred.one_liner: The football goal becomes the gender gap.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned abstract gender inequality statistics into tangible, unplayable football goals that sparked national conversation about women's treatment in Dutch sport, becoming ING's most engaging campaign worldwide.

visual.style_description: Cinematic outdoor photography of physical goal installations on a real pitch, paired with bold condensed display type and clean infographic callouts. Editorial case-study layout with dense supporting copy and supplementary thumbnails.

extracted.tagline: Guess Who Won - History favours those who get dirty
extracted.insight: When you look at famous moments in sports archives, the winners are the ones that got dirtier.
extracted.idea: An OOH campaign that recreates iconic sports moments, replicating the dirt stains on uniforms in each match down to the smallest detail, asking viewers to guess who won.
execution: Recreated OOH posters of iconic tennis, cricket and football moments with 'Guess Who Won' headline
Placements near schools, recreational parks and youth sports clubs in UK, India and Brazil
Reference thumbnails of original sports moments: The Ashes Final Day (2007), FA Women's League Cup (2023), Wimbledon Final (2008), Roland Garros Final (2016), Intercontinental Cup (1996)

inferred.context: OMO (Persil) is a laundry detergent brand whose long-running 'Dirt is Good' platform argues that getting dirty is part of growing, learning and achieving — a counter to parents' reluctance to let kids get messy during play and sport.
inferred.one_liner: The dirtier player is the winner.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned laundry stains into trophies by proving across iconic sports moments that the dirtier athlete always won, reinforcing OMO's 'Dirt is Good' platform and encouraging kids near schools and pitches to embrace mess as part of greatness.

visual.style_description: High-production sports photography shot from the waist down on a clay tennis court, with dirt-streaked uniforms hero'd against a saturated orange ground; supporting case images and reference thumbnails are arranged in a clean editorial grid with bold sans-serif typography.

extracted.tagline: Interactive live-generated ads that prove AI thinks "ketchup" looks like Heinz.
extracted.context: A year after Heinz's first AI text-to-image campaign proved AI sees ketchup as Heinz, image generators had advanced to near photorealistic renders. Heinz set out to prove that the better AI gets, the more it still thinks ketchup looks like Heinz.
extracted.idea: At SXSW, Heinz built an interactive board powered by ChatGPT-4 that invited tech experts to finish the prompt "ketchup bottle in...". Hundreds of AI-generated ads were created live, all resembling Heinz, then turned into OOH ads across major cities.
execution: Interactive kiosk at SXSW prompting users to finish 'Ketchup bottle in...'
Tablet/touchscreen interface for entering prompts
Live AI-generated posters shown to attendees on-site
Posters turned into OOH placements across major cities
Digital billboards and street-level OOH featuring AI-generated ketchup imagery
press_coverage: Fast Company: "A mighty flex, demonstrating the power of the Heinz brand."
The New York Times: "A.I. generates recognizable images of Heinz."
ABC: "Reflects the world's fascination with A.I."
Forbes: "Enhancing human intuition with machine intelligence."

inferred.insight: AI image generators, trained on the world's collective visual data, default to Heinz whenever asked to picture ketchup — proving Heinz's iconic status is now embedded in machine perception itself.
inferred.one_liner: Ketchup, according to A.I., is Heinz.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Heinz turned generative AI into a live proof point of its own iconicity, demonstrating that even machines see ketchup as Heinz. The campaign reinforced Heinz's category ownership at the world's biggest tech moment and earned massive global press validation.

visual.style_description: Case-board layout on deep Heinz red, anchored by a row of overlapping AI-generated poster mockups in wildly different artistic styles (stained glass, Van Gogh, photoreal diner, anime landscape). Dense but organized, with bold display type, press logos, and event documentation photos across the bottom.

extracted.context: Solmack, a top anti-hangover remedy brand in Japan, sought to enter the Singapore market. However, a new Health Science Authority regulation banned 'anti-hangover' claims in marketing materials.
extracted.idea: Launch Solmack in Singapore using a key visual that communicates the product's benefits without explicitly stating its anti-hangover function, deployed across multi-channel promotions in pubs, bars and KTV lounges.
execution: OOH posters and bus stop / street ads featuring the key visual
In-store displays at bars and liquor shops
Outdoor billboard placements at night-life venues
Point-of-sale bottle toppers with QR code
Branded merchandise (T-shirts) with the key visual
metrics: 873 boxes product pre-orders in a single night
3757 QR promo code scans in a single day

inferred.insight: In Singapore's nightlife culture, drinkers need a pre-emptive ritual — but regulation forbids naming the cure, so the brand must show the behaviour instead of the benefit.
inferred.one_liner: The bottle becomes the drink you take before the drink.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Sidestepped Singapore's ban on anti-hangover claims by encoding the product's function into a single wordless visual, successfully launching a Japanese brand into a new market and establishing a pre-drinking ritual among nightlife consumers.

visual.style_description: Bold flat-vector illustration of a figure tipping a bottle into their mouth, rendered in deep navy on a warm cream background; minimalist poster aesthetic deployed consistently across OOH, in-store and merchandise.

extracted.tagline: Turning a standard billboard into a 15 sqm sticky lint roller.

extracted.idea: IKEA set up a billboard in the middle of Oslo printed on sticky paper, turning a classic 2D billboard into a huge lint roller that Norwegians in bunads could roll themselves against.
execution: Giant sticky-surface billboard installed at Oslo's busiest railway station
Poster featured BÄSTIS lint roller product with price and instructions to 'roll dust off your finery here'
Norwegians in traditional bunads rolling themselves against the billboard
People in suits also using the sticky surface to clean their clothes
Visitors taking selfies in front of the installation

inferred.insight: Wool bunads worn on Norway's National Day are magnets for lint and dust, leaving Norwegians fussing over their finery in public.
inferred.one_liner: The billboard IS a giant lint roller.
inferred.qualitative_summary: IKEA inserted itself into Norway's most beloved national ritual by turning outdoor media into a functional product demo, letting citizens physically interact with the BÄSTIS lint roller at scale and reinforcing IKEA's role as a practical helper in everyday Norwegian life.

visual.style_description: Case-study layout with a large hero photograph of the sticky billboard in use, supported by a grid of documentary-style action shots and annotated callouts. Clean editorial typography on a subtle textured lint-paper background reinforces the product story.

extracted.tagline: The loss of freedom is never obvious at first. Trust the free press. Not pretty words.
extracted.context: 2024 is a pivotal year for democracy with over 80 countries holding elections. While some may gain more freedom, others face the risk of losing it. The free press is what can stand against the enemies of democracy.
extracted.insight: There is a gap between the beautiful promises of modern autocrats when first elected and the ugly truth they later inflict upon democracies.
extracted.idea: For its 30th anniversary, RSF Germany exposes the hopeful words used by modern autocrats in their first presidential speeches—Putin, Maduro, Erdoğan—juxtaposed with images of journalists persecuted shortly after.
execution: Long-copy print posters featuring first speeches of Putin (Russia 2000), Maduro (Venezuela 2013), and Erdoğan (Turkey 2014)
Digital out-of-home displays across Europe
Magazine spreads
Photography of persecuted journalists (e.g., journalist detained in Moscow 2012; journalist arrested in Istanbul 2021)
QR code driving donations

inferred.one_liner: Autocrats' first speeches sound like democracy's promises.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed the inaugural speeches of Putin, Maduro and Erdoğan as cautionary evidence, warning European voters in a pivotal election year that beautiful political rhetoric often precedes the dismantling of press freedom. Positioned RSF as the trusted counterweight to autocratic charm during its 30th anniversary.

visual.style_description: Stark black backgrounds with monospaced typewriter-style typography evoking redacted documents and journalistic transcripts, paired with grainy black-and-white photojournalism of persecuted reporters. Dense long-copy layouts mimic printed speeches, lending editorial gravitas.

extracted.tagline: How we got the most stubborn social group in Poland change their health habits

extracted.insight: Men don't care about their health, but they care about their cars a lot.
extracted.idea: Polish law requires every car to have a yearly check-up. We partnered with the largest car control station brand to let men check their livers while checking their cars.
execution: Partnership with the largest car control station chain in Poland for liver tests during vehicle check-ups
TV commercial featuring a man with car/liver issues
On-site testing at car inspection stations with dietitians/specialists
News/TV media coverage with on-location interviews (e.g. Klaudia Wiśniewska, dietitian)
Digital hub for liver health needs with social/editorial content
Partnerships with Young Timer Warsaw, Truckers Life, Speedcar and two famous race drivers

inferred.one_liner: The car check-up becomes the liver check-up.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed Poland's mandatory vehicle inspection into a men's health screening ritual, reaching a notoriously health-averse audience and driving tens of thousands of real liver tests. Shifted male health behaviour by piggybacking on a habit they already trusted: caring for their car.

visual.style_description: Split-composition poster pairing a mechanic and a doctor in mirrored poses, overlaid on a dark dashboard-gauge background with a liver-shaped warning icon. Cinematic photography with bold orange typography and tile-laid supporting stills.

extracted.context: Indonesia's hot, humid climate breeds flies that transmit 65+ diseases. Open-air school canteens leave children vulnerable; over 100 food poisoning cases in schools occurred in 2 years, with one student dying from E.Coli-linked diarrhoea in Bandung.
extracted.insight: Flies hate yellow — it is the number one colour that repels flies.
extracted.idea: Transform school canteens by painting tables, benches and walls yellow to repel flies and create a healthier dining environment for students.

Website for schools to register/nominate canteens needing transformation

inferred.one_liner: The canteen IS the fly repellent.

The initiative scaled through a nomination platform and earned global press as a simple, science-backed act of brand purpose.

extracted.tagline: We didn't sponsor their gaming. We sponsored their breaking.
extracted.context: Twitch streamers grind for hours, but when they take a break, their audience leaves the stream. An empty chair means lost eyeballs because fans want constant action 24/7.
extracted.insight: Fans hate breaks in streams — the moment a streamer leaves, viewers drop off.
extracted.idea: A scannable, one-of-a-kind gamer chair that rewarded fans for taking a break from streaming — The Break Chair. Sponsoring breaking, not gaming.
execution: Influencer partnerships with Loserfruit (3M followers) and snack-sized streamers (+1.3M combined) seated on The Break Chair
AFK (Away For KitKat): when streamers left the room, the chair revealed a giant hidden QR code on stream
Scan & Have A Break: fans scanned the code to access a custom landing page synced to the stream with a 3-minute countdown timer, winning limited-edition KitKats featuring their favorite streamers
Return From The Break: streamers came back refreshed and played better
Thumbnail strip showing streamers with custom KitKat bars and the red Break Chair in their setups

inferred.one_liner: The empty streamer chair becomes the ad.
inferred.qualitative_summary: KitKat turned streaming's most-hated moment — the break — into its most-anticipated, rewarding fans for stepping away and reinforcing the brand's 'Have a Break' platform inside gaming culture.
